title: Cat Swelling Under Eye: Causes, Urgency, and Care Guide
meta_desc: Noticed swelling under your cat's eye? Learn about common causes like tooth abscesses, the urgency of care, and how a photo helps your veterinarian.
content_html:
What is the swelling under my cat's eye?

Swelling in the suborbital region (under the eye) is a common clinical sign in cats. Most frequently, this is caused by a carnassial tooth root abscess, where an infection at the root of the upper fourth premolar breaks through the bone and creates a pocket of fluid under the skin. Other possibilities include a localized abscess from a bite wound or scratch, an allergic reaction to an insect sting, a blocked nasolacrimal duct, or occasionally, a cyst or tumor. Because the skin under the eye is very thin, inflammation in the mouth or sinus cavity often manifests here very clearly.

Is this an emergency?

The urgency level for this condition is categorized as Medium. In most cases, it is not an immediate life-threatening emergency, but it is a sign of significant pain and active infection. You should aim to have your cat seen by a veterinarian within 24 to 48 hours. If the swelling is accompanied by high fever, extreme lethargy, or if the eye itself is being pushed closed or looks cloudy, you should seek a same-day appointment to prevent the infection from spreading to the eye or bloodstream.

How a photo helps with triage

Capturing a clear, high-resolution photo of the swelling is one of the most helpful things you can do for your vet. A photo allows a professional to see the exact proximity of the swelling to the eye and whether there is any drainage or broken skin. It also provides a vital baseline; by comparing the photo to your cat's current state a few hours later, the vet can determine how rapidly the swelling is progressing, which helps them prioritize your cat's case during the triage process.

faqs.question: Can I give my cat human pain relief for the swelling?
faqs.answer: No. Never give your cat human medications like Tylenol (Acetaminophen) or Aspirin, as these are highly toxic to cats and can be fatal. Only use medications prescribed by your veterinarian.

faqs.question: Could the swelling be caused by a tooth issue even if my cat is still eating?
faqs.answer: Yes. Cats are experts at hiding pain and will often continue to eat even with a severe tooth root abscess. The swelling itself is often the only outward sign of a significant dental problem.

faqs.question: What should I do if the swelling starts to drain fluid?
faqs.answer: If the abscess bursts and drains, gently wipe away the discharge with a warm, damp cloth, but do not apply pressure or attempt to squeeze it. You still need to see a vet immediately, as the underlying cause (like a rotten tooth) remains and will reinfect the area.
